questions.question: What is the most rigorously studied construct underlying 'healthy boundaries'?

options.text: Codependency and enmeshment patterns
Assertiveness training with RCT evidence showing effect sizes of 0.95-1.73
Emotional intelligence and self-awareness
Personality traits like introversion and extroversion

questions.explanation: Assertiveness is the most extensively researched construct with rigorous RCT evidence. Internet-based assertiveness training shows large effect sizes (ES 0.95–1.73) at one-year follow-up. While other constructs are studied, assertiveness has the strongest intervention evidence base.

questions.question: What is the key difference between healthy boundaries and isolating walls?

options.text: Walls are temporary during trauma recovery; boundaries are permanent patterns
Boundaries allow connection while preserving autonomy; walls prevent all intimacy
Boundaries are for relationships; walls are only for strangers
There is no meaningful difference — they're the same thing

questions.explanation: Healthy boundaries are permeable and values-based — they allow genuine connection while preserving your autonomy. Walls are rigid and fear-based, preventing all intimacy and creating isolation. However, walls can be temporarily adaptive during trauma recovery or for immediate safety.

questions.question: Which communication style is characterized by saying 'yes' when you want to say 'no' and minimizing your own feelings?

options.text: Assertive communication
Passive communication
Aggressive communication
Values-based communication

questions.explanation: Passive communication involves difficulty expressing needs, saying 'yes' when you mean 'no,' and minimizing your own feelings. This pattern often stems from fear of conflict, cultural messages about selfishness, or history of punishment for self-expression. The consequence is accumulated resentment and unbalanced relationships.

questions.question: What does research show about the claim that 'setting boundaries keeps relationships healthy'?

options.text: It's fully supported — communication skills alone predict long-term relationship satisfaction
It's an overstatement — communication improves relationship process but doesn't predict long-term satisfaction longitudinally
It's completely false — boundaries damage relationships
It only applies to romantic relationships, not family or friendships

questions.explanation: Longitudinal research (Lavner et al.) shows that while assertive communication improves how relationships function (reduced escalation, increased clarity), communication skills alone don't strongly predict long-term relationship satisfaction. Compatibility, attachment security, and values alignment matter just as much. The popular claim oversimplifies relationship complexity.

questions.question: When is assertiveness training NOT sufficient as a standalone approach for boundary difficulties?

options.text: When you're in an abusive or coercively controlling relationship where communication can escalate danger
When you're naturally introverted and prefer alone time
When you're in your first romantic relationship and still learning
Assertiveness training is always sufficient for any boundary problem

questions.explanation: In abusive or coercively controlling relationships, assertiveness training is contraindicated as a primary intervention — it can escalate danger. Safety planning, advocacy, and specialist support are needed instead. Additionally, boundary difficulties rooted in severe trauma histories (C-PTSD, complex attachment wounds) require therapeutic processing, not just skill instruction. The course explicitly provides referral pathways for these situations.

title: What Boundaries Actually Are — and Are Not - Quiz
